title: Racette Lab: Promoting Cardiometabolic Health & Healthspan

summary.hook: This poster is a lab portfolio overview summarizing four ongoing/related research areas in the Racette Lab focused on cardiometabolic health, Alzheimer’s disease-related...
summary.summary_30s: This poster is a lab portfolio overview summarizing four ongoing/related research areas in the Racette Lab focused on cardiometabolic health, Alzheimer’s disease-related interventions, Native American cardiovascular health, and healthspan-extension approaches.

key_numbers: Not stated on poster (XPRIZE described as 7-year competition).
Study #1 TREAD Trial is registered as ClinicalTrials.gov NCT06429124.
Study #4 references XPRIZE Healthspan as a 7-year, $101 million global competition and lists intervention modalities...
It highlights (1) a time-restricted eating feasibility trial in mild cognitive impairment (MCI) with a...
takeaways: This poster primarily presents aims, methods, sites, collaborators, and study identifiers rather than numeric outcomes.
Study #1 reports “Study Design & Preliminary Results” but directs viewers to “See poster by Jordan Gunning, PhD” for details.
Study #4 describes XPRIZE Healthspan as a 7-year, $101 million global competition and lists intervention methods used by the ASU team.

general: What is the main takeaway you hope attendees remember from this poster?
What surprised you most once you started digging into this topic?
Why does this work matter beyond this specific poster session?
undergrad: What part of this project would be most helpful for an undergraduate student to learn from?
What skills or tools were most important for getting this project done?
If an undergraduate wanted to get involved in work like this, where would you suggest they start?
grad: For the TREAD trial (Study #1), how is TRE operationalized (eating window length, timing) and what counts as “feasibility”?
What was the key design or analysis tradeoff in this project?
If you were extending this into a next study, what would you tighten or measure differently?
researcher: For the TREAD trial (Study #1), how is TRE operationalized (eating window length, timing) and what counts as “feasibility”?
What cardiometabolic and cognitive measures are included in Study #1 beyond what’s diagrammed in the mechanistic model?
In Study #2, what “physiological pathways” are you measuring acutely that relate to amyloid beta deposition and neurogenesis?
clinician: How could these findings affect clinical care, screening, or decision-making in practice?
What kind of patient or care setting would this poster be most relevant for?
What would need to happen before this work would be ready for broader clinical use?
community: Who in the community is most likely to benefit if this work moves forward?
What kinds of community partnerships would strengthen the next phase of this project?
What would meaningful real-world impact look like for the people touched by this work?
leader: What type of support, infrastructure, or investment would help this work move further?
What is the clearest implementation or scale-up opportunity that comes out of this poster?
If a leader wanted to back the next step, what outcome would be most important to track?
